teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Inheritance tax (IHT) is governed by the Inheritance Tax Act 1984 (IHTA 1984). It is charged on the value transferred by a chargeable transfer — primarily on death (the deceased's estate) and on certain lifetime transfers. The tax is charged at 40% on the value of the estate above the nil-rate band, with a reduced rate of 36% where 10% or more of the net estate is left to charity.

**The Charge on Death:** On death, the deceased is treated as making a transfer of value equal to the value of their estate immediately before death (IHTA 1984, s.4). The estate includes all assets to which the deceased was beneficially entitled — property, investments, cash, personal possessions, and certain trust interests. The personal representatives are liable for the IHT on the estate and must pay it before obtaining a grant of probate (or face interest and penalties).

**Lifetime Transfers:** Not all lifetime transfers are immediately chargeable. The legislation distinguishes between: (a) potentially exempt transfers (PETs) — gifts to individuals (or certain trusts for disabled persons) that are exempt if the donor survives 7 years. If the donor dies within 7 years, the PET becomes a chargeable transfer and is taxed at the death rates, subject to taper relief; (b) chargeable lifetime transfers (CLTs) — transfers into most types of trust, where IHT is charged at the lifetime rate of 20% immediately and additional tax may be due on death within 7 years.

**The 7-Year Rule:** The 7-year survivorship period is central to IHT planning. Gifts made more than 7 years before death are generally outside the estate for IHT purposes. Gifts within 7 years are added back and taxed, with taper relief reducing the tax after 3 years (from 100% of the full death-rate tax at 0–3 years, reducing by 20% per year until year 6–7 where only 20% of the full tax is payable).

**In short:** IHT is charged on the value of the estate on death and on certain lifetime transfers. PETs become exempt after 7 years. CLTs are chargeable at 20% when made, with potential further tax on death. The executor calculates the tax on the total chargeable estate at 40% above the nil-rate band.

question: The primary charging Act for inheritance tax is the Inheritance ______ Act 1984 (IHTA 1984), which charges tax on the value transferred by a chargeable transfer.
feedback: Correct. Inheritance Tax Act 1984 — IHTA 1984 is the principal consolidating Act. It charges IHT on the value transferred on death (s.4) and on chargeable lifetime transfers (s.2), subject to exemptions and reliefs.

question: Under IHTA 1984, s.____, on death, the deceased is treated as making a transfer of value equal to the value of their estate immediately before death. The tax is charged as if the deceased had made that transfer.
feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.4 — the deemed transfer on death. The personal representatives must value the estate (all assets to which the deceased was beneficially entitled) as at the date of death and pay the IHT before probate is granted.

question: Match each transfer type or concept to its correct treatment under IHTA 1984:
answer: Potentially Exempt Transfer (PET)=A gift to an individual — exempt if the donor survives 7 years; Chargeable Lifetime Transfer (CLT)=A gift into most types of trust — IHT charged immediately at 20% (lifetime rate); Transfer on death=The deceased's estate is valued and charged at 40% above the nil-rate band; Taper relief=Reduces the IHT on failed PETs or additional tax on CLTs where the donor survives between 3 and 7 years after the transfer

options.text: A gift to an individual — exempt if the donor survives 7 years
A gift into most types of trust — IHT charged immediately at 20% (lifetime rate)
The deceased's estate is valued and charged at 40% above the nil-rate band
Reduces the IHT on failed PETs or additional tax on CLTs where the donor survives between 3 and 7 years after the transfer

feedback: Well matched. The distinction between PETs (gifts to individuals — potentially exempt) and CLTs (gifts to trusts — immediately chargeable) is fundamental to IHT planning. Taper relief softens the 7-year rule: if the donor dies 5 years after the gift, the tax is reduced to 40% of the full amount.

question: In 2018, Margaret gave £100,000 to her daughter to help her buy a house. Margaret died in January 2025 (just over 6 years later). Margaret's nil-rate band had been fully used by other gifts made shortly before the £100,000 gift. What is the IHT treatment of this gift?

options.text: The gift is exempt because it was made more than 5 years before death
options.feedback: Incorrect. The survivorship period is 7 years, not 5. At just over 6 years, the gift is still within the 7-year period, though taper relief applies.

options.text: IHT at 40% on the full £100,000 — because Margaret died within 7 years
options.feedback: Partially correct that the gift is chargeable, but taper relief applies. At 6–7 years, only 20% of the full rate tax is due.

options.text: The gift is a failed PET (Margaret died within 7 years), so IHT is charged. However, taper relief applies: with 6+ years survival, the tax payable is 20% of the full rate. Since the nil-rate band is exhausted, the effective rate is 40% × 20% = 8% on £100,000, so £8,000 IHT due
options.feedback: Correct. The gift is a failed PET. Taper relief: 6–7 years = 20% of the full death-rate tax. Since the nil-rate band had been exhausted, the full rate is 40%. Effective rate: 40% × 20% = 8%. Tax: £100,000 × 8% = £8,000. Note: taper relief applies to the tax, not the value of the gift.

options.text: The gift is exempt because it was a gift to her daughter, and all gifts to children are exempt from IHT
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no general exemption for gifts to children. Gifts to children are PETs — exempt only if the donor survives 7 years. Specific exemptions may apply (annual £3,000, normal expenditure out of income, gifts on marriage) but these are separate.

feedback: The interaction of the 7-year rule and taper relief is a core IHT concept. Taper relief reduces the tax (not the value) on a sliding scale after 3 years: 3–4 years = 80% of full tax, 4–5 years = 60%, 5–6 years = 40%, 6–7 years = 20%. Margaret's gift at just over 6 years attracts only 20% of the full tax. The nil-rate band is applied to the earliest transfers first (the 7-year cumulation rule).

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'All gifts made by an individual during their lifetime are immediately chargeable to inheritance tax at 20%. If the donor dies within 7 years, additional tax is charged at 40%. There is no distinction between gifts to individuals and gifts to trusts — both are treated identically for IHT purposes.'
answer: Not all lifetime gifts are immediately chargeable. Gifts to individuals are potentially exempt transfers (PETs) — no tax is due when the gift is made, and it becomes exempt if the donor survives 7 years. Gifts into trusts are typically chargeable lifetime transfers (CLTs) — taxed immediately at 20% with potential additional tax on death within 7 years. The treatment of PETs and CLTs is fundamentally different.
feedback: Found multiple errors. The key distinction is between PETs (gifts to individuals — no immediate charge) and CLTs (gifts to trusts — immediate charge at 20%). PETs are only taxable retrospectively if the donor dies within 7 years. The statement conflates the two completely separate transfer types.

question: Which TWO of the following are correct statements about the charge to inheritance tax under IHTA 1984?

options.text: IHT is charged on the entire value of the deceased's estate with no tax-free allowance
options.feedback: Incorrect. The nil-rate band of £325,000 means the first £325,000 of the estate is taxed at 0%. Only the value above the nil-rate band (and available residence nil-rate band) is charged at 40%.

options.text: A gift to an individual is a potentially exempt transfer — it becomes exempt from IHT if the donor survives 7 years after making the gift
options.feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.3A — a PET is exempt if the donor survives 7 years. If the donor dies within 7 years, the PET becomes chargeable and is taxed at the death rates (subject to taper relief).

options.text: Taper relief reduces the value of the gift that is included in the estate
options.feedback: Incorrect. Taper relief (IHTA 1984, s.7(4)) reduces the tax payable, not the value of the gift. The gift is included at its full value; the tax on it is reduced by the applicable taper percentage.

options.text: On death, the personal representatives are liable for IHT on the deceased's estate and must generally pay before a grant of probate is issued
options.feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.4 — the estate is valued at the date of death, and the personal representatives must account for and pay the IHT. Probate cannot generally be obtained until the IHT account is delivered and at least some tax paid.

options.text: If a PET fails because the donor dies within 7 years, the tax is payable by the recipient of the gift, not the estate
options.feedback: Partially correct but not fully. The primary liability for IHT on a failed PET falls on the donee (recipient), but the personal representatives also have secondary liability. If the donee does not pay, HMRC can recover from the estate.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). The PET/7-year rule is the foundation of lifetime IHT planning. The personal representatives' duty to pay before probate underscores why life insurance written in trust is so common — it provides liquidity outside the estate. Taper relief reduces the tax, not the value; a common trap for the unwary.

question: Under IHTA 1984, s.3A, a __________ exempt transfer is a gift made by an individual to another individual (or certain trusts for disabled persons) that becomes exempt from IHT if the donor survives for 7 years after the gift.
feedback: Correct. A potentially exempt transfer (PET) — the 'potentially' is key. The transfer is exempt if-and-only-if the donor survives 7 years. If the donor dies sooner, the PET becomes chargeable and is taxed, subject to taper relief after 3 years.

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Not all of the deceased's estate is charged to IHT. The nil-rate band (NRB) and residence nil-rate band (RNRB) provide significant tax-free thresholds.

**Nil-Rate Band (NRB):** IHTA 1984, Sch.1 — the nil-rate band is £325,000. The first £325,000 of the chargeable estate is taxed at 0%. The NRB has been frozen at £325,000 since 2009 and is scheduled to remain at this level until April 2028. Each individual has their own NRB, and any unused NRB from a deceased spouse or civil partner may be transferred to the survivor (IHTA 1984, s.8A). The maximum transferable NRB is 100% of the NRB at the survivor's death. This means a married couple can together pass up to £650,000 tax-free (assuming neither has used their NRB for lifetime transfers).

**Residence Nil-Rate Band (RNRB):** IHTA 1984, s.8D–8M — an additional allowance introduced in 2017, the residence nil-rate band is available where a residence (or the proceeds of sale of a former residence) is left to direct descendants (children, grandchildren, etc.). For the 2024/25 tax year, the RNRB is £175,000. Like the NRB, any unused RNRB can be transferred to a surviving spouse or civil partner. The RNRB is tapered: it is reduced by £1 for every £2 that the deceased's net estate exceeds £2 million (tapering to zero at £2.35 million). Where the estate exceeds £2 million, careful planning is required.

**Combined Thresholds:** A married couple/civil partners with children, each with an unused NRB and RNRB, can pass up to £1 million tax-free to direct descendants: £325k + £175k (first death, typically transferred) + £325k + £175k (second death) = £1 million. Above this, the rate is 40%.

**In short:** Each individual has a £325,000 nil-rate band. A further £175,000 residence nil-rate band applies where a home passes to direct descendants. Both are transferable between spouses. The combined maximum tax-free threshold for a couple with children is £1 million.

question: Under IHTA 1984, Sch.1, the __________-rate band is £325,000 — the first £325,000 of an individual's chargeable estate is taxed at 0%.
feedback: Correct. The nil-rate band is £325,000 and has been frozen at this level since 2009, with the freeze extended to April 2028 (announced in the 2022 Autumn Statement). Any unused NRB can be transferred to a surviving spouse or civil partner.

question: The __________ nil-rate band (RNRB) under IHTA 1984, ss.8D–8M provides an additional allowance of up to £175,000 where a residence is left to direct descendants, such as children or grandchildren.
feedback: Correct. The residence nil-rate band is an additional £175,000 (2024/25) available when a qualifying residential interest passes to direct descendants. It is tapered for estates valued over £2 million. Transfers between spouses/civil partners can carry forward the unused RNRB.

question: Place the IHT thresholds in ascending order for a married couple with children where both spouses have full unused NRB and RNRB:
answer: First spouse's nil-rate band — £325,000 → First spouse's residence nil-rate band — £175,000 (transferable) → Total transferred to second spouse — up to £500,000 → Second spouse's nil-rate band — £325,000 → Second spouse's residence nil-rate band — £175,000 → Combined tax-free threshold on second death — up to £1,000,000

options.text: First spouse's nil-rate band: £325,000 (transferred if unused)
First spouse's residence nil-rate band: £175,000 (transferred if unused)
Total transferred to surviving spouse: up to £500,000
Surviving spouse's own NRB: £325,000
Surviving spouse's own RNRB: £175,000
Combined tax-free threshold on second death: up to £1,000,000

feedback: Correct. For many families, the key to the 'million-pound IHT-free estate' is the transfer of both the NRB and RNRB from the first spouse to the survivor. This planning is automatic (the personal representatives claim the transfer on the survivor's death) and does not require a specific will provision — though the RNRB conditions must still be met (residence passing to direct descendants).

question: In 2025, widower Arthur dies. His estate is worth £900,000 and includes his house (valued at £400,000) left to his two daughters. Arthur's late wife died in 2018 leaving her entire estate to Arthur; she made no lifetime gifts and no NRB or RNRB was used on her death. What are the available IHT thresholds on Arthur's death?

options.text: Nil-rate band only: £325,000. RNRB cannot transfer from a spouse
options.feedback: Incorrect. Unused RNRB is transferable from a deceased spouse just as the NRB is (IHTA 1984, s.8D(5)(g)).

options.text: Nil-rate band: £650,000 (Arthur's £325,000 + transferred wife's £325,000). Residence nil-rate band: £350,000 (Arthur's £175,000 + transferred wife's £175,000). Total tax-free threshold: £1,000,000. Arthur's £900,000 estate is below this — no IHT is due
options.feedback: Correct. Both the NRB and RNRB are transferable. Arthur benefits from his own NRB (£325k), his late wife's unused NRB (£325k), his own RNRB (£175k), and his late wife's unused RNRB (£175k). Total: £1,000,000. His £900,000 estate is fully covered. The house passes to direct descendants, satisfying the RNRB condition.
Incorrect. You have omitted the transferred NRB. Both thresholds are transferable.
Incorrect. Both the NRB and RNRB are transferable from a spouse who died without using them. The full transferable amounts should be added.

feedback: The transferability of both the NRB and RNRB is the reason a married couple can pass up to £1 million to their children tax-free. Both must be claimed within 2 years of the survivor's death by the personal representatives. Arthur's estate at £900,000 is within the combined £1,000,000 threshold — no IHT is payable on his death.

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'The residence nil-rate band is available to all estates regardless of who inherits the property. It is a flat £175,000 addition for every estate that includes a residential property, with no restrictions on the value of the estate or the identity of the beneficiaries. It is not transferable between spouses because it is linked to the property, not the person.'
answer: The RNRB has three important restrictions: (1) it is only available where the residence passes to direct descendants (children, grandchildren, etc.) — not to any beneficiary; (2) it is tapered for estates over £2 million, reducing to zero at £2.35 million; (3) it IS transferable between spouses/civil partners (IHTA 1984, s.8D(5)(g)). The transfer is personal, not property-linked.
feedback: Found multiple errors. The RNRB is targeted: it must pass to 'direct descendants' (children, grandchildren, step-children, etc. — IHTA 1984, s.8K). It is tapered above £2 million. And contrary to the paragraph, it IS transferable between spouses — the transferable RNRB relates to the percentage of the RNRB unused by the first spouse, not to a specific property.

question: Which TWO of the following are correct about the residence nil-rate band under IHTA 1984?

options.text: The RNRB is available where a residence is left to direct descendants (children, grandchildren, or their spouses)
options.feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.8K — the residence must pass to 'direct descendants' which includes children, grandchildren, step-children, adopted children, and the spouses/civil partners of those descendants.

options.text: The RNRB applies automatically to all estates that include a residential property, regardless of beneficiaries
options.feedback: Incorrect. The RNRB is conditional: the residence must be left to direct descendants. If the property is left to a sibling, charity, or non-descendant, the RNRB does not apply (unless downsizing provisions are relevant).

options.text: The RNRB is £325,000 — the same as the standard nil-rate band
options.feedback: Incorrect. The RNRB is £175,000 for 2024/25. The standard NRB is £325,000. They are separate allowances that can be combined.

options.text: The RNRB is tapered where the deceased's net estate exceeds £2 million, reducing by £1 for every £2 of excess, reaching zero at £2.35 million
options.feedback: Correct. The RNRB taper is a critical planning point for estates approaching £2 million. The NRB is not affected by this taper — only the RNRB is withdrawn.

options.text: If the residence is worth less than £175,000, the unused balance of the RNRB is lost and cannot be used against other assets
options.feedback: Partially correct but misleading. If the residence is worth less than the RNRB, the downsizing provisions (IHTA 1984, s.8FA–8FE) may allow the unused RNRB to apply where the deceased disposed of a more valuable former residence. The balance is not automatically lost, but it can only be used in limited circumstances.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (d). The RNRB is €175,000 of additional tax-free allowance, but only where the residence passes to direct descendants. The taper above £2 million is a deliberate policy to limit the benefit for wealthier estates. The downsizing provisions add complexity for those who moved to a smaller property after 8 July 2015.

question: Under IHTA 1984, s.____, any unused nil-rate band of a deceased spouse or civil partner may be transferred to the survivor, potentially doubling the tax-free threshold on the survivor's death.
feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.8A — the transferable nil-rate band. The percentage of the NRB unused by the first spouse is applied to the NRB in force at the survivor's death. For example, if the first spouse used 60% of their NRB, 40% of the NRB at the survivor's death is transferred.

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** IHTA 1984 provides specific exemptions and reliefs that reduce the chargeable value of a transfer. The most important are the spouse/civil partner exemption, the charity exemption, the annual exemption, and the normal expenditure out of income exemption.

**Spouse/Civil Partner Exemption:** IHTA 1984, s.18 — transfers between spouses and civil partners (whether on death or during lifetime) are wholly exempt from IHT, provided both parties are domiciled in the UK (or the transferor is UK-domiciled within the 7-year lookback rule). There is no monetary limit on this exemption. However, if the recipient spouse is non-UK domiciled, the exemption is capped at the nil-rate band (£325,000) unless an election to be treated as UK-domiciled is made. This exemption is the single most powerful IHT planning tool — married couples can pass their entire estate to each other tax-free, deferring IHT until the survivor's death.

**Charity Exemption:** IHTA 1984, s.23 — gifts to charities (whether during lifetime or on death) are exempt from IHT. Additionally, if 10% or more of the net estate is left to charity, the IHT rate on the remainder is reduced from 40% to 36% (IHTA 1984, Sch.1A).

**Annual Exemption:** IHTA 1984, s.19 — each individual may give away up to £3,000 per tax year free of IHT. Unused annual exemption from the previous tax year can be carried forward for one year only (so up to £6,000 is available if the prior year's allowance was not used). This applies to lifetime gifts and reduces the value of PETs or CLTs made in that year.

**Normal Expenditure Out of Income:** IHTA 1984, s.21 — regular gifts made out of income (not capital) that do not affect the donor's standard of living are exempt. There is no monetary limit, but the donor must demonstrate a pattern of regular giving and that the gifts are made from surplus income after meeting usual living expenses. This is often used for whole-of-life insurance premiums paid to a trust.

**In short:** The four key IHT exemptions are: spouse/civil partner (unlimited), charity (unlimited, with rate reduction incentive), annual £3,000 (plus one year's carry-forward), and normal expenditure out of income (unlimited, but must be from surplus income).

question: Under IHTA 1984, s.____, transfers between spouses and civil partners are wholly exempt from IHT, whether made during lifetime or on death, provided both are domiciled in the UK.
feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.18 — the spouse/civil partner exemption. This is unlimited in value. For non-UK domiciled recipients, the exemption is capped at the nil-rate band (£325,000) unless an election is made.

question: Under the IHTA 1984, s.____ exemption, an individual may give away up to £3,000 in total each tax year free of IHT, with any unused allowance carried forward for one tax year only.
feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.19 — the annual exemption. £3,000 per tax year. The exemption applies to the total gifts in the year; if gifts exceed £3,000, the exemption is applied to the earliest gifts first. Unused exemption from the prior year can be carried forward, giving a potential £6,000 in any one year.

question: Match each IHT exemption to its correct description and limit:
answer: Spouse/civil partner exemption=Unlimited — all transfers to UK-domiciled spouse/civil partner are exempt; Charity exemption=Unlimited — gifts to qualifying charities are exempt, and if 10%+ of net estate goes to charity, the IHT rate reduces to 36%; Annual exemption=£3,000 per tax year, with one year's unused carry-forward (max £6,000); Normal expenditure out of income=Unlimited — regular gifts from surplus income that do not affect the donor's standard of living

options.text: Unlimited — all transfers to UK-domiciled spouse/civil partner are exempt
Unlimited — gifts to qualifying charities are exempt, and if 10%+ of net estate goes to charity, the IHT rate reduces to 36%
£3,000 per tax year, with one year's unused carry-forward (max £6,000)
Unlimited — regular gifts from surplus income that do not affect the donor's standard of living

feedback: Well matched. The spouse exemption and charity exemption are truly unlimited (subject to domicile rules for spouses). The annual exemption is modest but reliable. Normal expenditure out of income is powerful but requires evidence — executors must demonstrate the gifts were regular, from income, and left the donor's standard of living unaffected.

question: Anthony, a widower, dies in 2025 with an estate valued at £800,000. His will leaves £100,000 to Cancer Research UK (a qualifying charity). The remainder goes to his son. Anthony's nil-rate band of £325,000 is fully available, and no RNRB applies. What is the IHT liability?

options.feedback: Incorrect. The charity legacy of £100,000 is exempt and should be deducted from the estate. Also, the 10% charity threshold may reduce the rate to 36%.
Partially correct in the computation of the chargeable estate, but you have missed the charity rate reduction. The 10% test may apply.

options.text: IHT at 36% on £375,000 = £135,000. The charity legacy of £100,000 is exempt. The chargeable estate is £375,000. Since the charitable legacy (£100,000) exceeds 10% of the net estate, the reduced rate of 36% applies to the remainder
options.feedback: Correct. Step 1: deduct exempt charity legacy = £100,000. Step 2: net estate = £800,000 − £100,000 = £700,000. Step 3: 10% of net estate = £70,000. The charity legacy (£100,000) exceeds this, so the 36% rate applies. Step 4: chargeable estate = £700,000 − £325,000 (NRB) = £375,000. Step 5: £375,000 × 36% = £135,000. This saves £15,000 compared to the standard 40% rate.

options.text: No IHT is due because Anthony left more than 10% to charity
options.feedback: Incorrect. The charity exemption exempts the legacy from tax, and the 10% rule reduces the rate on the remainder to 36%. It does not exempt the entire estate. Only the charitable gift itself is exempt.

feedback: The charity rate reduction (IHTA 1984, Sch.1A) is a clever incentive: by leaving 10%+ of the net estate to charity, the effective IHT rate on the remainder drops from 40% to 36%. This both reduces IHT and benefits the chosen charity. The 10% test is applied to the 'baseline amount' — broadly, the net estate after deductions but before charitable legacies.

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'All gifts to charity are subject to inheritance tax because charities are not individuals. The annual exemption of £3,000 is available per gift, so a donor can give multiple gifts of £3,000 to different people in the same year all covered by the exemption. The spouse exemption is capped at the nil-rate band of £325,000.'
answer: Three errors. (1) Gifts to qualifying charities are wholly exempt (IHTA 1984, s.23) — they are not subject to IHT. (2) The annual exemption of £3,000 applies to the total of all gifts in the tax year, not per gift — it is an aggregate exemption. (3) For UK-domiciled recipients, the spouse exemption is unlimited — it is not capped at the nil-rate band. The £325,000 cap applies only where the recipient spouse is non-UK domiciled.
feedback: Found multiple errors. Charity gifts are exempt. The annual £3,000 exemption covers total gifts in the year (with the oldest gifts getting the benefit first). The spouse exemption is unlimited for UK-domiciled recipients — it is one of the most powerful IHT exemptions.

question: Which TWO of the following are correct statements about IHT exemptions under IHTA 1984?

options.text: Gifts made as normal expenditure out of income are exempt from IHT, provided they are regular, made from surplus income, and do not affect the donor's standard of living
options.feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.21 — the normal-expenditure-out-of-income exemption. No monetary limit, but the executors must prove the pattern and that the gifts came from income, not capital. This exemption is often claimed for regular payments into trust for life insurance premiums.

options.text: The annual exemption of £3,000 applies to each individual gift, allowing multiple £3,000 gifts per year
options.feedback: Incorrect. The £3,000 annual exemption applies to the total gifts in the year. If total gifts exceed £3,000, the excess is chargeable (subject to other exemptions).

options.text: Gifts between UK-domiciled spouses or civil partners are exempt from IHT without any monetary limit
options.feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.18 — the spouse/civil partner exemption is unlimited. It applies both during life and on death. This is the cornerstone of most IHT planning for married couples.

options.text: The charity exemption only applies to legacies on death, not to lifetime gifts
options.feedback: Incorrect. IHTA 1984, s.23 — gifts to charities are exempt whether made during lifetime or on death. A donor may make charitable gifts during life free of IHT.

options.text: The annual exemption can be carried forward indefinitely if unused
options.feedback: Incorrect. The annual exemption can be carried forward for one tax year only. Unused exemption from more than one year ago is lost.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). The normal expenditure exemption is underused but powerful — it requires careful record-keeping. The spouse exemption is the most valuable: an estate can be passed entirely tax-free between spouses, deferring IHT until the survivor's death. The annual exemption is modest (£3,000 total per year, one-year carry-forward only).

question: George, aged 72, has surplus income of £15,000 per year after meeting his living expenses. He has regularly given £10,000 per year to his son for the past 6 years to help with mortgage payments. George dies in 2025. The £10,000 annual gifts continue until his death. How are these gifts treated for IHT purposes?

options.text: All £60,000 is added back as failed PETs — the 7-year rule applies to each year's gift separately
options.feedback: Incorrect. While the gifts within 7 years would ordinarily be failed PETs, the normal-expenditure-out-of-income exemption (s.21) applies first. Gifts qualifying under s.21 are exempt, not PETs.

options.text: All gifts are exempt under the normal-expenditure-out-of-income exemption (IHTA 1984, s.21) — they were regular, from surplus income, and did not affect George's standard of living. They do not form part of his chargeable estate or use the nil-rate band
options.feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, s.21 — the gifts meet all conditions: (1) regular pattern (annual for 6 years), (2) made from income (not capital — his surplus income covered them), and (3) his standard of living was unaffected. The exemption is unlimited in amount; no IHT is due on these gifts. The gifts also do not count against the nil-rate band on death.

options.text: Only £3,000 per year is exempt under the annual exemption — the remaining £7,000 per year is a failed PET and added back to the estate
options.feedback: Incorrect. The annual exemption and the normal-expenditure-out-of-income exemption are separate and can both apply. However, where gifts qualify under s.21, the annual exemption is not exhausted — it remains available for other gifts.

options.text: The gifts are exempt because they were made to George's son, and all gifts to children are exempt from IHT
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no blanket exemption for gifts to children. The exemption here arises from s.21 (normal expenditure out of income), not from the identity of the recipient.

feedback: The normal-expenditure-out-of-income exemption is one of the most effective IHT planning tools. It is often used to fund regular payments (school fees, insurance premiums, mortgage support) for adult children without eroding the nil-rate band and without needing to survive 7 years. The three conditions — regularity, from income, no impact on standard of living — must be evidenced, typically by the executors completing form IHT403.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] On her 70th birthday in 2020, Catherine gave £50,000 to her granddaughter as a wedding gift. Catherine died in March 2025 (almost 5 years later). Catherine also made no other lifetime gifts above the annual exemption. Advise on the IHT position.

options.text: The gift is exempt — gifts on marriage are always exempt from IHT
options.feedback: Incorrect. The marriage exemption is limited: £5,000 from a parent to a child on marriage, £2,500 from a grandparent, £1,000 from anyone else (IHTA 1984, s.22). Only £2,500 of the £50,000 is covered by the marriage exemption.

options.text: The full £50,000 is a failed PET — IHT at 40% applies because Catherine died within 7 years
options.feedback: Partially correct that it is a failed PET, but you have omitted the annual exemptions and taper relief. The gift is reduced by £6,000 (two years' annual exemptions) and taper relief applies at just under 5 years.

options.text: This is a failed PET (Catherine died within 7 years). Deduct: £2,500 marriage exemption + £6,000 annual exemptions (£3,000 × 2 years). Net PET: £41,500. Taper relief at 4–5 years = 60% of full tax. Nil-rate band applies first — if the NRB (£325,000) covers the £41,500, no IHT is due. If the NRB was used by other gifts, the tax on £41,500 at 40% × 60% taper = £9,960
options.feedback: Correct. The marriage exemption (£2,500 for grandparent) and two years of annual exemption (£6,000) reduce the PET to £41,500. With 4–5 years' survival, taper relief reduces the tax to 60% of the full rate. The nil-rate band is applied to the earliest transfers first (the 7-year cumulation rule). If Catherine's NRB can accommodate this, no tax is due anyway.

options.text: The gift is exempt because it was made more than 4 years before death and was for a wedding
options.feedback: Incorrect. The 7-year survivorship period must be fully satisfied for a PET to become exempt. 5 years is not enough. The wedding only justifies a £2,500 exemption for a grandparent-grandchild gift.

feedback: This consolidates Block A: the key elements are the nature of a PET (exempt after 7 years but chargeable on death within that period), the availability of specific exemptions (marriage and annual), and the application of taper relief (reducing the tax, not the value) after 3 years. Always deduct available exemptions first, then apply the nil-rate band cumulatively across all transfers in the 7-year period.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] Under IHTA 1984, Sch.1, the nil-rate band is £325,000. An unused nil-rate band from a deceased __________ may be transferred to the survivor, potentially doubling the tax-free allowance on the survivor's death.
feedback: Correct. The transferable nil-rate band (IHTA 1984, s.8A) applies to spouses and civil partners. The percentage of NRB unused on the first death is applied to the NRB in force at the survivor's death. The RNRB is also transferable (ss.8D–8M).

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] A husband and wife own their home jointly worth £700,000 and have other assets of £500,000 between them. They have two children. Their wills leave everything to each other on the first death, and then to the children on the second death. Both are UK-domiciled and neither has made significant lifetime gifts. What is the likely IHT position on the first death?

options.text: IHT at 40% on the first spouse's half-share of the estate — the spouse exemption does not apply to jointly owned property
options.feedback: Incorrect. The spouse exemption applies to all transfers to a UK-domiciled spouse, including jointly owned property passing by survivorship.

options.text: IHT at 20% — the lifetime rate applies because spouses inherit during life (through survivorship), not on death
options.feedback: Incorrect. Transfers on death to a spouse are at the death rate (which is 0% because of the spouse exemption). The 20% lifetime rate applies to CLTs, not to death transfers.

options.text: No IHT is due on the first death — the spouse exemption (IHTA 1984, s.18) exempts all transfers to a UK-domiciled spouse, including the deceased's share in the matrimonial home passing by survivorship or under the will
options.feedback: Correct. The spouse exemption exempts the entire transfer on the first death, whether passing by will, intestacy, survivorship, or nomination. The first spouse's NRB and RNRB (both unused) will be claimable on the second death, giving the children a combined threshold of up to £1 million. No IHT is due until the second death.

options.text: IHT is due on the house (above the RNRB) at 40%, but the other assets are exempt under the spouse exemption
options.feedback: Incorrect. The spouse exemption applies to all assets passing to the surviving spouse, not just some. The RNRB is irrelevant on the first death because the spouse exemption covers everything.

feedback: This consolidates Block C: the spouse exemption (s.18) is the key to deferring IHT. On the first death, all assets passing to the surviving spouse are exempt. The NRB and RNRB are preserved (transferred as a percentage) for the second death. The children will inherit with the benefit of both parents' combined allowances. This is the standard 'nil-rate band discretionary trust' will structure or, more simply, an outright spousal transfer.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] A single individual dies in 2025 with an estate of £500,000, including a house worth £300,000 left to her son. She made a PET of £100,000 to her niece 4 years before death. Her nil-rate band is £325,000. Which TWO of the following are correct?

options.text: The PET of £100,000 is exempt because 4 years is enough to qualify for the reduced rate under taper relief
options.feedback: Incorrect. Taper relief reduces the tax but does not exempt the gift. The PET failed because the donor died within 7 years. It is chargeable but subject to taper relief (60% of full tax at 4–5 years).

options.text: The nil-rate band of £325,000 is applied to the PET first (as the earliest transfer in the 7-year period), leaving £225,000 available for the death estate
options.feedback: Correct. The 7-year cumulation rule applies: the nil-rate band is allocated to transfers in chronological order. The £100,000 PET consumes £100,000 of the NRB, leaving £225,000 for the death estate.

options.text: The entire £500,000 estate is charged at 40% above the £325,000 NRB, ignoring the PET
options.feedback: Incorrect. The NRB must be apportioned: £100,000 to the PET, £225,000 remaining for the estate. Only £275,000 of the estate is chargeable. Also, the RNRB may apply because the house passes to the son.

options.text: The residence nil-rate band does not apply because this is a single individual with no spouse to transfer the allowance
options.feedback: Incorrect. The RNRB (ss.8D–8M) is available to individuals in their own right — it does not require a deceased spouse. The transferable RNRB is in addition to an individual's own RNRB. The condition for the individual's own RNRB is that a residence passes to direct descendants.

options.text: The RNRB of £175,000 applies because the house passes to a direct descendant (son), providing additional relief above the remaining NRB
options.feedback: Correct. IHTA 1984, ss.8D–8M — the RNRB applies because the house passes to the son (a direct descendant). The RNRB is £175,000. Combined threshold for the death estate: remaining NRB £225,000 + RNRB £175,000 = £400,000. The estate is £500,000 but £100,000 was the PET. The death estate IHT is on £500,000 − £400,000 = £100,000 at 40% = £40,000.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (e). This consolidates all three blocks: the failed PET (Block A), the nil-rate band cumulation rule (Block B), and the RNRB where a home passes to direct descendants (Block B). The computation on death: (1) apply exemptions to the PET — £100,000, (2) apply NRB to PET first, (3) calculate remaining NRB for estate, (4) apply RNRB, (5) charge remaining estate at 40%. The total IHT includes the tax on the PET (after taper) plus the tax on the estate. Taper relief on the PET: at 4–5 years survival, the tax on £100,000 is 40% × 60% × £100,000 = £24,000, payable by the niece.

question:
[CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'An individual can give away unlimited sums each year under the normal-expenditure-out-of-income exemption. This exemption covers all gifts made out of capital as well as income, as long as the donor's standard of living is unaffected. The 7-year survivorship rule applies to all lifetime gifts, including those covered by the spouse exemption, which means a gift to a spouse is taxable if the donor dies within 7 years.'
answer: Three errors. (1) The normal-expenditure-out-of-income exemption applies only to gifts made out of surplus INCOME, not capital (IHTA 1984, s.21). (2) The 7-year rule does NOT apply to exempt transfers — the spouse exemption (s.18) is absolute; a transfer to a spouse is exempt whether the donor lives 7 years or 7 minutes. The 7-year PET rule applies to gifts that are NOT otherwise exempt. (3) The spouse exemption is immediate and complete — there is no survivorship requirement.
feedback: Found multiple errors. The s.21 exemption requires gifts from income (not capital), and it is the donee/executors who must prove this. The 7-year survivorship rule applies to PETs — transfers that are not otherwise exempt. The spouse exemption is wholly exempt from the moment of transfer; there is no 7-year condition. This is a critical distinction: a PET is 'potentially' exempt; a spouse transfer IS exempt.
